lessons.body: console.log appends a newline, while process.stdout.write writes exactly the bytes you give it.
lessons.example:
const score: number = 7;
process.stdout.write(`example:score=${score}\n`);
lessons.title: Console and stdout

lessons.body: const protects a binding from reassignment; let marks the few local values that intentionally change.
lessons.title: let and const

lessons.body: string, number, and boolean describe the common scalar values that most stdin parsing produces.
lessons.title: Primitive types

lessons.body: Template literals keep formatting close to the values, and string methods return new strings instead of mutating text.
lessons.title: Strings and templates

lessons.body: number[] models a sequence of same-shaped values; a tuple fixes both position and type for small records.
lessons.title: Arrays and tuples

lessons.body: Object type annotations name required fields so calculations cannot silently ignore missing properties.
lessons.title: Object types

lessons.body: Parameter and return annotations make the input and output contract visible at the call site.
lessons.title: Functions

lessons.body: In coding tests, read fd 0 once, split the text into tokens, and convert tokens before doing numeric work.
lessons.title: Node stdin parsing

lessons.body: if, for, while, and switch all narrow the path values can take before they reach stdout.
lessons.title: Control flow

lessons.body: A union accepts several shapes, but TypeScript only allows member-specific operations after a runtime check narrows the value.
lessons.title: Union and narrowing

lessons.body: Literal unions restrict values to exact strings or numbers, which is useful for modes, commands, and states.
lessons.title: Literal types

lessons.body: Optional properties read as possibly undefined, and ?? keeps valid falsey values such as 0 or an empty string.
lessons.title: Optional and nullish

lessons.body: Interfaces and aliases both name object contracts; aliases also name unions, tuples, and type expressions.
lessons.title: Interfaces and type aliases

lessons.body: Generics let reusable functions preserve the caller's type instead of collapsing values to any.
lessons.title: Generics

lessons.body: typeof captures the static type of a value, and keyof turns that object type into a union of valid keys.
lessons.title: keyof and typeof

lessons.body: Indexed access types read a property type from another type so value code and type code stay in sync.
lessons.title: Indexed access types

lessons.body: Mapped types loop over keys at the type level, often to turn one object shape into another related shape.
lessons.title: Mapped types

lessons.body: Conditional types choose one type branch from another type, and infer can capture part of a matched shape.
lessons.title: Conditional types

lessons.body: Utility types such as Pick, Partial, Required, and Awaited express common transformations without custom aliases.
lessons.title: Utility types

lessons.body: A shared literal field lets switch narrow each variant and makes missing cases visible during type checking.
lessons.title: Discriminated unions

lessons.body: async functions return Promise values; await unwraps the fulfilled value before later code uses it.
lessons.title: Async and Promise

lessons.body: catch receives an unknown failure; narrow it before reading Error-specific fields or choosing a fallback.
lessons.title: Error handling

lessons.body: import and export make file boundaries explicit; in Node type stripping, module syntax still follows Node's module rules.
lessons.title: Modules and exports

lessons.body: Classes combine state with methods; TypeScript access modifiers describe the intended boundary for that state.
lessons.title: Classes and access modifiers

lessons.body: readonly documents that callers may read a property or array but should not replace or mutate it through that type.
lessons.title: readonly

lessons.body: as const preserves literal values, and satisfies checks a wider contract without widening the value's own type.
lessons.title: satisfies and as const

lessons.body: for...of consumes any iterable, so arrays, strings, sets, and many Node values can share loop code.
lessons.title: Iterables

lessons.body: map transforms each item, filter keeps selected items, and reduce folds a sequence into one accumulated value.
lessons.title: map, filter, and reduce
